era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period. Athens is engaged in multiple military campaigns simultaneously: the Egyptian expedition continues, tensions with Sparta are escalating, and democratic reforms are consolidating. The Delian League is becoming increasingly centralized under Athenian control. Sparta is preparing for conflict with Athens. Egypt remains under Persian control with ongoing rebellion. China's Spring and Autumn period continues.

events.title: Battle of Tanagra (First Peloponnesian War begins)
events.description: A major hoplite battle is fought at Tanagra in Boeotia between an Athenian-allied force and a Spartan-led coalition. The battle is fiercely contested and results in a Spartan victory, though at significant cost. The battle marks the opening of the First Peloponnesian War (461–446 BCE) or represents a major escalation of existing tensions. Despite the defeat, Athens maintains its imperial position and continues its campaigns elsewhere.
key_figures: Spartan commanders (names not preserved in sources)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.107

events.certainty_note: The battle of Tanagra is well-attested in later sources, but its precise date is debated. Thucydides places it during the First Peloponnesian War but does not provide a specific year. Scholars variously date it to 458, 457, or 456 BCE. The assignment to 458 BCE is conventional based on the broader chronology.

events.title: Athenian victory at Oenophyta and consolidation of Boeotian control
events.description: Shortly after the defeat at Tanagra, the Athenian general Myronides defeats a Boeotian force at Oenophyta, reasserting Athenian control over Boeotia and the surrounding region. This victory demonstrates Athenian resilience and the effectiveness of its hoplite forces. The victory consolidates Athenian dominance in central Greece and secures the land route to the Peloponnese.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.108

events.certainty_note: The battle of Oenophyta is attested in Thucydides and later sources. It is typically dated to shortly after Tanagra, so if Tanagra is 458 BCE, Oenophyta would also be 458 BCE or possibly 457 BCE.

events.title: Assassination of Ephialtes
events.description: Ephialtes, the radical democratic reformer who stripped the Areopagus of its powers, is assassinated by conservative opponents. The assassination is a significant political event, removing a key democratic leader and marking the consolidation of Pericles' political dominance. The exact circumstances and perpetrators are not recorded in surviving sources, but the assassination reflects the bitter factional struggles within Athens.

sources.name: Aristotle, Constitution of the Athenians, Chapter 25

events.certainty_note: Ephialtes' assassination is attested in later sources, but the precise date is uncertain. Scholars place it between 460 and 456 BCE, with 458 or 457 BCE being conventional assignments. Aristotle does not provide a specific date.

events.title: Athenian Egyptian campaign continues amid Persian resistance
events.description: The Athenian expedition to Egypt, now in its third year, faces increasing Persian military pressure. The campaign is becoming logistically difficult and politically contentious in Athens. However, Athenian forces maintain their presence and continue to support the Egyptian rebel Inarus. The campaign will collapse within a few years.
key_figures: Cimon (Athenian commander, though possibly recalled by this year)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.104

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the campaign is inferred from the broader chronology. No specific events are recorded for 458 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Tanagra is highly contested. Some scholars place it in 457 or 456 BCE rather than 458 BCE. The relationship between Tanagra and Oenophyta is also debated; some sources suggest they occurred in the same year, others place them years apart. Ephialtes' assassination is variously dated to 460, 458, 457, or 456 BCE. The significance of Tanagra as a Spartan victory is accepted, but the strategic consequences are debated—some scholars argue it had little lasting impact on Athenian power.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 458 BCE is dominated by Thucydides' summary account and later compilations. No contemporary Athenian inscriptions or documents are known to survive for this year. The Egyptian perspective is almost entirely absent. The dating of events in this period is conventional and subject to significant scholarly debate. The sources are biased toward Athenian perspectives and may overstate Athenian military successes.

graph_edges.note: The defeat at Tanagra is followed by Athenian victory at Oenophyta
Ephialtes' assassination follows from his democratic reforms
The Egyptian campaign continues toward its eventual failure
